title: Stored Procedures and Functions
description: Creating and calling stored procedures, user-defined functions, and output parameters

concepts.title: Creating Stored Procedures

sqlServer.description: SQL Server stored procedures are created with CREATE PROCEDURE and use T-SQL as the procedural language. They support input parameters with default values, SET NOCOUNT ON to suppress row-count messages, and structured error handling via BEGIN TRY / BEGIN CATCH. Procedures are called with EXEC and return result sets directly to the caller.
notes: SET NOCOUNT ON prevents 'N rows affected' messages from being sent to the client, improving performance in loops.
Use IF OBJECT_ID(...) IS NOT NULL to safely drop before recreating, or use CREATE OR ALTER PROCEDURE (SQL Server 2016+).
Procedures can return multiple result sets by including multiple SELECT statements.
The RAISERROR function re-throws the caught error with its original severity and state.

postgres.description: PostgreSQL introduced CREATE PROCEDURE in version 11, but the traditional approach uses CREATE OR REPLACE FUNCTION with RETURNS TABLE for query results. All routines require a LANGUAGE declaration — most commonly plpgsql for procedural code. Procedures are called with CALL; functions are invoked in a SELECT. Unlike SQL Server, PostgreSQL procedures cannot return result sets directly, so a function with RETURNS TABLE is the standard pattern for returning rows.
keyDifferences: PostgreSQL requires LANGUAGE plpgsql — there is no implicit procedural language.
PostgreSQL procedures (CREATE PROCEDURE) cannot return result sets; use functions with RETURNS TABLE instead.
Use CALL to invoke procedures; use SELECT to invoke functions.
CREATE OR REPLACE eliminates the need to drop and recreate.
Error handling uses RAISE EXCEPTION with ERRCODE instead of RAISERROR with severity/state.
Dollar-quoting ($$) is used instead of single quotes for the function body to avoid escaping issues.
notes: CREATE PROCEDURE was added in PostgreSQL 11. On older versions, use functions for everything.
RAISE NOTICE sends an informational message to the client (equivalent to PRINT in SQL Server).
USING ERRCODE sets the SQLSTATE code for the exception, enabling precise error handling by callers.
The plpgsql language is bundled with PostgreSQL and requires no separate installation.

concepts.keyDifference: PostgreSQL procedures cannot return result sets — use functions with RETURNS TABLE for query output. All routines require an explicit LANGUAGE declaration, and CALL is used to invoke procedures while SELECT invokes functions.

concepts.title: Output Parameters and Return Values

sqlServer.description: SQL Server procedures use the OUTPUT keyword on parameters to pass values back to the caller. The RETURN statement sends an integer status code (separate from output parameters). Callers must declare variables, pass them with the OUTPUT keyword, and read them after the EXEC call. The EXEC statement can capture the RETURN value by assigning it: EXEC @RetVal = dbo.ProcName.
notes: OUTPUT parameters must be passed with the OUTPUT keyword at both the declaration and call site.
RETURN can only pass an integer. For other data types, use OUTPUT parameters.
Convention is RETURN 0 for success and non-zero for error conditions.
The OUTPUT keyword can be abbreviated as OUT.

postgres.description: PostgreSQL uses OUT parameters declared in the function signature. When a function has OUT parameters, its return type is automatically a record containing those columns — no RETURNS clause is needed. The function is called like a table (SELECT * FROM fn()) and each OUT parameter appears as a column. There is no separate RETURN integer concept; status codes are simply added as additional OUT parameters.
keyDifferences: PostgreSQL OUT parameters are declared in the function signature, not the body.
A function with OUT parameters returns a record — call it with SELECT * FROM fn() to see all values.
There is no RETURN integer status code concept; add a status OUT parameter instead.
PostgreSQL uses := for variable assignment inside plpgsql blocks.
OUT parameter values are automatically returned when the function exits — no explicit RETURN of the values needed.
notes: A function with only OUT parameters implicitly returns a record type — the RETURNS clause is optional.
Inside a plpgsql function, OUT parameters are treated as regular local variables that get returned automatically.
Use SELECT ... INTO to populate OUT parameters from a query.
The DO block is PostgreSQL's equivalent of an anonymous batch — useful for testing without creating a permanent function.

concepts.keyDifference: PostgreSQL OUT parameters are part of the function signature and returned as a record row — there is no separate RETURN integer mechanism. Call functions with OUT params using SELECT * FROM fn() to retrieve all output values as columns.

concepts.title: Scalar User-Defined Functions

sqlServer.description: SQL Server scalar user-defined functions (UDFs) are created with CREATE FUNCTION and return a single value via a RETURNS data_type clause. The function body is procedural T-SQL enclosed in BEGIN/END. Scalar UDFs must be called with their schema prefix (e.g., dbo.fn_FormatFullName) in SQL queries. They can be used anywhere a scalar expression is valid: SELECT lists, WHERE clauses, computed columns.
notes: SQL Server scalar UDFs can be performance-intensive when used on large tables because they execute once per row without parallelism (prior to SQL Server 2019 scalar UDF inlining).
SQL Server 2019+ introduced scalar UDF inlining, which can automatically convert simple scalar UDFs into inline expressions for better performance.
Always qualify function calls with the schema prefix (dbo.fn_Name) or the call will fail.
T-SQL scalar functions cannot contain DML (INSERT/UPDATE/DELETE) or execute stored procedures.

postgres.code:
-- Create a scalar function in PostgreSQL using plpgsql
DROP FUNCTION IF EXISTS fn_format_full_name(VARCHAR, VARCHAR, VARCHAR);

CREATE OR REPLACE FUNCTION fn_format_full_name(
    p_first_name  VARCHAR(100),
    p_last_name   VARCHAR(100),
    p_format      VARCHAR(20) DEFAULT 'LAST_FIRST'
)
RETURNS VARCHAR(205)
LANGUAGE plpgsql
AS $$
BEGIN
    IF p_format = 'LAST_FIRST' THEN
        RETURN p_last_name || ', ' || p_first_name;
    ELSIF p_format = 'FIRST_LAST' THEN
        RETURN p_first_name || ' ' || p_last_name;
    ELSE
        RETURN p_first_name || ' ' || p_last_name;
    END IF;
END;
$$;

-- Use the function in a SELECT (no schema prefix required for public schema)
SELECT
    fn_format_full_name('Jane', 'Smith', 'LAST_FIRST') AS last_first,
    fn_format_full_name('Jane', 'Smith', 'FIRST_LAST') AS first_last;

-- Use in a table query
SELECT
    customer_id,
    fn_format_full_name(
        split_part(full_name, ' ', 1),
        split_part(full_name, ' ', 2)
    ) AS formatted_name
FROM public.customer_profiles;

-- SQL-language scalar function (simpler, better optimized for pure SQL)
CREATE OR REPLACE FUNCTION fn_format_full_name_sql(
    p_first_name  VARCHAR,
    p_last_name   VARCHAR,
    p_format      VARCHAR DEFAULT 'LAST_FIRST'
)
RETURNS VARCHAR
LANGUAGE sql
IMMUTABLE STRICT
AS $$
    SELECT CASE p_format
        WHEN 'LAST_FIRST' THEN p_last_name || ', ' || p_first_name
        ELSE p_first_name || ' ' || p_last_name
    END;
$$;
postgres.description: PostgreSQL scalar functions use RETURNS <type> and require a LANGUAGE declaration. For simple expressions, LANGUAGE sql is preferred over plpgsql because it allows the optimizer to inline the function. For conditional logic, plpgsql is appropriate. No schema prefix is needed for functions in the public schema (which is on the default search_path). The IMMUTABLE marking tells the optimizer the function always returns the same result for the same inputs, enabling query optimizations like index use.
keyDifferences: No schema prefix required when calling functions in the public schema (search_path handles resolution).
LANGUAGE sql functions can be inlined by the optimizer — prefer for simple expressions over plpgsql.
IMMUTABLE, STABLE, and VOLATILE markings control optimizer behavior; SQL Server has no equivalent annotations.
PostgreSQL uses ELSIF (not ELSE IF) in plpgsql conditional blocks.
String concatenation uses || operator (same as SQL Server +, but || also works for nulls differently).
STRICT causes the function to return NULL immediately if any input is NULL, without executing the body.
notes: LANGUAGE sql scalar functions are often faster than plpgsql because the planner can inline them into the calling query.
IMMUTABLE means the function's result depends only on its arguments and never changes — enables caching and index use.
STRICT (or CALLED ON NULL INPUT by default) controls NULL handling: STRICT returns NULL if any argument is NULL.
split_part(string, delimiter, n) is the PostgreSQL equivalent of splitting a string and taking the nth part.

concepts.keyDifference: PostgreSQL functions do not require a schema prefix in calls (public schema is on the default search_path), and LANGUAGE sql functions can be inlined by the optimizer for better performance. Use IMMUTABLE to hint that the function result is deterministic.

concepts.title: Table-Valued Functions

sqlServer.code:
-- Inline Table-Valued Function (ITVF) — best performance, treated like a view
IF OBJECT_ID('dbo.fn_GetCustomersByLanguage', 'IF') IS NOT NULL
    DROP FUNCTION dbo.fn_GetCustomersByLanguage;
GO

CREATE FUNCTION dbo.fn_GetCustomersByLanguage
(
    @LanguageCode NVARCHAR(10),
    @CountryCode  NVARCHAR(10) = NULL
)
RETURNS TABLE
AS
RETURN
(
    SELECT
        CustomerID,
        Username,
        FullName,
        CountryCode,
        LanguageCode
    FROM dbo.CustomerProfiles
    WHERE LanguageCode = @LanguageCode
      AND (@CountryCode IS NULL OR CountryCode = @CountryCode)
);
GO

-- Use the ITVF like a table
SELECT CustomerID, Username, FullName
FROM dbo.fn_GetCustomersByLanguage('EN', NULL)
ORDER BY FullName;

-- Filter results from the TVF
SELECT *
FROM dbo.fn_GetCustomersByLanguage('EN', 'US')
WHERE FullName LIKE 'S%';

-- Multi-Statement TVF (MSTVF) — less efficient, use only when ITVF is not possible
IF OBJECT_ID('dbo.fn_GetCustomerSummary', 'TF') IS NOT NULL
    DROP FUNCTION dbo.fn_GetCustomerSummary;
GO

CREATE FUNCTION dbo.fn_GetCustomerSummary
(
    @CountryCode NVARCHAR(10)
)
RETURNS @Result TABLE
(
    CountryCode  NVARCHAR(10),
    CustomerCount INT,
    Languages    NVARCHAR(MAX)
)
AS
BEGIN
    INSERT INTO @Result
    SELECT
        CountryCode,
        COUNT(*),
        STRING_AGG(DISTINCT LanguageCode, ', ')
    FROM dbo.CustomerProfiles
    WHERE CountryCode = @CountryCode
    GROUP BY CountryCode;

    RETURN;
END;
GO

SELECT * FROM dbo.fn_GetCustomerSummary('US');
sqlServer.description: SQL Server has two types of table-valued functions: Inline TVFs (ITVF) which contain a single SELECT in a RETURNS TABLE AS RETURN clause and are treated like parameterized views by the optimizer, and Multi-Statement TVFs (MSTVF) which declare a table variable and populate it across multiple statements. ITVFs are strongly preferred for performance. Both types are queried with FROM dbo.fn_Name(params).
notes: Inline TVFs are optimized like views — the optimizer can push predicates, use statistics, and produce parallel plans.
Multi-Statement TVFs prevent most optimizer optimizations and should be avoided when an inline TVF is possible.
TVFs can be joined to other tables in the FROM clause just like regular tables.
APPLY operators (CROSS APPLY, OUTER APPLY) are commonly used with TVFs to call them per-row.

postgres.code:
-- PostgreSQL RETURNS TABLE function (equivalent to SQL Server ITVF)
DROP FUNCTION IF EXISTS fn_get_customers_by_language(VARCHAR, VARCHAR);

CREATE OR REPLACE FUNCTION fn_get_customers_by_language(
    p_language_code  VARCHAR(10),
    p_country_code   VARCHAR(10) DEFAULT NULL
)
RETURNS TABLE (
    customer_id   INT,
    username      VARCHAR,
    full_name     VARCHAR,
    country_code  CHAR,
    language_code CHAR
)
LANGUAGE plpgsql
AS $$
BEGIN
    RETURN QUERY
    SELECT
        cp.customer_id,
        cp.username,
        cp.full_name,
        cp.country_code,
        cp.language_code
    FROM public.customer_profiles cp
    WHERE cp.language_code = p_language_code::CHAR
      AND (p_country_code IS NULL OR cp.country_code = p_country_code::CHAR);
END;
$$;

-- Call it like a table
SELECT customer_id, username, full_name
FROM fn_get_customers_by_language('EN', NULL)
ORDER BY full_name;

-- Filter results from the function
SELECT *
FROM fn_get_customers_by_language('EN', 'US')
WHERE full_name LIKE 'S%';

-- SQL-language set-returning function (most efficient — optimizer can inline)
CREATE OR REPLACE FUNCTION fn_get_customers_by_language_sql(
    p_language_code  VARCHAR,
    p_country_code   VARCHAR DEFAULT NULL
)
RETURNS TABLE (
    customer_id   INT,
    username      VARCHAR,
    full_name     VARCHAR,
    country_code  CHAR
)
LANGUAGE sql
STABLE
AS $$
    SELECT
        cp.customer_id,
        cp.username,
        cp.full_name,
        cp.country_code
    FROM public.customer_profiles cp
    WHERE cp.language_code = p_language_code::CHAR
      AND (p_country_code IS NULL OR cp.country_code = p_country_code::CHAR);
$$;

-- RETURNS SETOF — returns rows of an existing type (alternative syntax)
CREATE OR REPLACE FUNCTION fn_get_all_customers_for_country(
    p_country_code VARCHAR
)
RETURNS SETOF public.customer_profiles
LANGUAGE sql
STABLE
AS $$
    SELECT * FROM public.customer_profiles
    WHERE country_code = p_country_code::CHAR;
$$;

SELECT customer_id, username FROM fn_get_all_customers_for_country('US');

-- LATERAL join (equivalent to SQL Server CROSS APPLY)
SELECT DISTINCT cp.country_code, lang.username
FROM public.customer_profiles cp
CROSS JOIN LATERAL fn_get_customers_by_language('EN', cp.country_code::VARCHAR) lang;
postgres.description: PostgreSQL set-returning functions (SRFs) use RETURNS TABLE or RETURNS SETOF to return multiple rows. They are called in the FROM clause just like SQL Server TVFs. LANGUAGE sql SRFs are the most efficient because the optimizer can inline them. LANGUAGE plpgsql SRFs use RETURN QUERY to emit rows. The LATERAL join syntax (CROSS JOIN LATERAL) is equivalent to SQL Server's CROSS APPLY for calling functions per-row.
keyDifferences: RETURNS TABLE and RETURNS SETOF are the PostgreSQL equivalents of SQL Server table-valued functions.
LANGUAGE sql SRFs are inlined by the optimizer, giving them performance similar to SQL Server inline TVFs.
Use RETURN QUERY inside plpgsql to emit a result set from a query.
LATERAL is the SQL standard keyword for what SQL Server calls CROSS APPLY / OUTER APPLY.
RETURNS SETOF <table_type> returns rows matching an existing table or composite type definition.
No schema prefix needed for public schema functions — just call fn_name() directly.
notes: STABLE tells the optimizer the function returns the same results within a single transaction for the same inputs — enables better planning.
RETURNS SETOF existing_table returns full rows of that table type, useful when you want all columns.
PostgreSQL does not have a separate 'multi-statement TVF' concept — plpgsql functions handle complex logic natively.
LATERAL allows functions to reference columns from preceding FROM items, enabling per-row function calls.
RETURN QUERY EXECUTE allows dynamic SQL within a set-returning function for advanced cases.

concepts.keyDifference: PostgreSQL set-returning functions (RETURNS TABLE / RETURNS SETOF) are the equivalent of SQL Server TVFs. Use LANGUAGE sql for maximum optimizer efficiency (equivalent to SQL Server inline TVFs). LATERAL replaces SQL Server's CROSS APPLY / OUTER APPLY syntax.
